stories.title: Braves Power Past Guardians — Acuña, Olson, Harris All Go Deep

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Ronald Acuña Jr. ✓
Matt Olson ✓
Michael Harris II ✓

Three different Braves went deep Friday night at Truist Park.

Atlanta wins, moves to 9-5. That lineup is LOADED. 🔥

#BallparkBanter #Braves #MLB

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Braves have three players in their lineup who can go deep any night.

Acuña. Olson. Harris. All homered Friday.

Drake Baldwin leads MLB with 15 RBIs. As a ROOKIE.

Atlanta is for real. 👀

#BallparkBanter #Braves #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 8:30 AM ET*

Three home runs from three different Braves. That's how Atlanta handled Cleveland on Friday night at Truist Park.

Ronald Acuña Jr., Matt Olson, and Michael Harris II all went deep off Cleveland's Slade Cecconi, giving the Braves an offensive showcase that their fanbase has been waiting to see. The Braves improved to 9-5 with the win, while the Guardians dropped to 8-6.

What makes this impressive isn't just the homer count — it's the depth behind it. The Braves have built a lineup where three different players can carry an offense on any given night. When the middle of your lineup is firing like this, you're a legitimate threat.

And then there's Drake Baldwin, who continues to lead the majors in RBIs. Atlanta isn't just talented at the top — they're stacked.

Watch for the Braves in May. This team has the look of a pennant-race participant.
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with Truist Park action photo*

Three home runs. Three Braves. One dominant Friday night. Acuña, Olson, and Harris powered Atlanta past Cleveland.

stories.title: Yankees Fall to Rays 5-3 — Luis Gil's Debut Struggles

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Luis Gil's 2026 debut: uneven.

Yandy Díaz punished a hanging slider — two-run homer, game over.

Yankees drop to 8-4. Two losses in a row after starting 8-2.

The dominant ERA was real. The struggles? Also real. ⚾

#BallparkBanter #Yankees #MLB

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Yankees 8-4 after losing to the Rays.

They started 8-2 with team ERA under 2.00.

Now two straight losses.

The rotation without Cole and Rodon is a real question mark. ⚾

#BallparkBanter #Yankees #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 12:00 PM ET*

The Yankees went from 8-2 to 8-4. Two losses in a row. It was bound to happen eventually.

Luis Gil made his 2026 season debut at Tropicana Field on Friday night, and it did not go as planned. The right-hander was uneven, and Yandy Díaz took advantage of a hanging slider — launching a go-ahead two-run homer that swung the game Tampa Bay's way. Ben Rice kept New York's hopes alive with a late homer, but the Rays held on, 5-3.

The Yankees had arguably the most dominant pitching start in franchise history through their first seven games — team ERA under 2.00, just eight runs allowed across seven games. That kind of run was always going to normalize. Two losses doesn't erase the real quality in that rotation.

But Gil's debut is a legitimate concern. The Yankees are without Cole and Rodon, and their depth is being tested. If Gil's not ready to pitch like a No. 3 or No. 4 starter, the rotation math gets harder.

This is a team that's still 8-4. Still well ahead of the pack in the AL East. But Friday was a reminder: no lead or start is bulletproof.
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with Yankees game photo*

Luis Gil's 2026 debut goes sideways. Yandy Díaz goes deep. Rays 5, Yankees 3. New York drops to 8-4.

stories.title: Max Muncy Hits 3 Home Runs as Dodgers Beat Rangers

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
Shohei Ohtani was intentionally walked Friday night.

Max Muncy responded by hitting THREE home runs.

The Dodgers aren't just built around Ohtani. They are DEEP. 🔥

#BallparkBanter #Dodgers #MLB

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
Max Muncy: 3 HR in one game.

The Dodgers beat the Rangers on Ohtani Bobblehead Night.

Muncy wasn't even supposed to be the headline. He became it. ⚾

#BallparkBanter #Dodgers #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 1:00 PM ET*

You know a lineup is dangerous when your fourth-best hitter is the one putting on a show.

Max Muncy slugged three home runs on Friday night at Dodger Stadium as the Los Angeles Dodgers defeated the Texas Rangers in the opener of a three-game series. It was Ohtani Bobblehead Night — but it ended up being Muncy's night.

Shohei Ohtani was intentionally walked at one point during the game, which tells you everything you need to know about how the Dodgers are perceived by opposing pitchers. You'd rather put Ohtani on first than face him with men on base. The problem? When you pitch around Ohtani, you still have to face Muncy. And Muncy will not be pitching around.

Three home runs in one game. Tyler Glasnow handled the pitching. Dodgers win.

The Dodgers continue to look like the class of the National League — and possibly all of baseball. Their depth, their rotation, and their lineup make them the team that every World Series contender has to worry about come October.
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with Dodger Stadium action photo*

Max Muncy goes yard three times. Ohtani gets intentionally walked. The Dodgers are a different kind of problem for everyone in baseball.

stories.title: Jose Ramirez Named AL Player of the Month — 300/300 Club Beckons

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
April 2026: Jose Ramirez

.342 BA. .722 SLG.
7 home runs. 28 RBIs.

AL Player of the Month.

He's at 292 career HR. 13 steals from 300/300 club history.

Still overlooked. Still elite. ⚾

#BallparkBanter #Guardians #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 5:30 PM ET*

If you're still sleeping on Jose Ramirez, April 2026 should be your wake-up call.

The Cleveland Guardians' third baseman was named the American League Player of the Month for April after one of the best single-month stretches of his career: a .342 batting average, .722 slugging percentage, seven home runs, and 28 RBIs in 21 games. Those are MVP-level numbers for a full month.

But the bigger story is where Ramirez sits historically. He entered the 2026 season with 285 career home runs and 287 career stolen bases. With seven April home runs, he's closing in on 292 career HRs. He needs just 13 stolen bases to reach 300 for his career.

The 300/300 club — 300 home runs and 300 stolen bases — is one of the rarest achievements in baseball history. Only eight players have done it: Bobby Bonds, Barry Bonds, Steve Finley, Carlos Beltran, Reggie Sanders, Jeff Bagwell, Larry Walker, and Alex Rodriguez. Ramirez is chasing history with every game he plays.

He's already one of the best players in franchise history. A healthy 2026 season could put him in conversation with some of the greatest switch-hitters to ever play the game.
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with Ramirez at bat photo*

Seven HRs. 28 RBIs. AL Player of the Month. Jose Ramirez is chasing the exclusive 300/300 club — and closing in fast.

stories.title: Drake Baldwin's Historic Start — First Brave to Score in 7 Straight Games Since Hank Aaron (1957)

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Drake Baldwin scored a run in each of the Braves' first 7 games.

The last Brave to do that was Hank Aaron — in 1957.

He's also leading MLB with 15 RBIs. As a rookie. 🔥

#BallparkBanter #Braves #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 3:30 PM ET*

Not many players get compared to Hank Aaron in their second week of the season. Drake Baldwin now has that distinction.

The Atlanta Braves rookie scored a run in each of the team's first seven games of the 2026 season — and he became the first Brave to accomplish that since Hank Aaron did it to open the 1957 season. That's not a stat you see very often.

In addition to the Aaron comparison, Baldwin's production has been elite by any measure: a .321 batting average, five home runs, and 15 RBIs through just 13 games — good enough to lead all of Major League Baseball in RBIs.

What makes Baldwin's start so impressive is that rookie players almost always need an adjustment period. The jump from Triple-A to the majors comes with a learning curve. So far, Baldwin hasn't shown any signs of that curve. He's hitting for average, hitting for power, and driving in runs at a rate that most veteran hitters don't sustain.

The Braves needed someone to step up and be a catalyst alongside Acuña, Olson, and Harris. They found one. Whether Baldwin can keep this pace for 162 games is the question — but two weeks in, he's making a case that no one expected.
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with Baldwin batting action photo*

15 RBIs. First Brave to score in 7 straight season openers since Hank Aaron in 1957. Drake Baldwin is not your average rookie.

stories.title: Red Sox Depths Deepen — Historically Bad Start Continues

x_posts.label: Post 6
x_posts.text:
The Red Sox are 2-8.

Worst record in baseball.

Tied for the worst nine-game franchise start in 126 YEARS.

They brought in Ranger Suárez, Sonny Gray, and more this offseason.

Alex Cora: "Unacceptable." ⚾

#BallparkBanter #RedSox #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 6:30 PM ET*

When your manager calls your team's performance "unacceptable," you know things are bad. When your record ties a 126-year franchise low, you know things are historically bad.

That's where the Boston Red Sox find themselves two weeks into the 2026 season.

Boston went out this offseason and made moves — adding Ranger Suárez, Sonny Gray, Willson Contreras, and Caleb Durbin, among others. The expectation was a bounce-back season after winning 89 games in 2025. Instead, they're sitting at 2-8, the worst record in Major League Baseball, with a pattern of losses that is difficult to fix quickly.

Five losses have come by two runs or fewer. They are 0-3 in one-run games. The close losses pile up and the run differential reflects a team that competes, but doesn't convert. That's often a sign of bullpen and clutch-hitting problems — neither of which has an easy overnight fix.

Alex Cora has been around long enough to know when to sound the alarm. He's sounded it.

The Red Sox season isn't over. Baseball is long, and teams have recovered from worse starts. But every day that ticks off the calendar at 2-8 makes the math harder.
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with Fenway photo*

2-8. Worst record in baseball. Tied for the worst nine-game start in Red Sox history in 126 years. Something has gone very wrong in Boston.

stories.title: MLB Week 2 Power Rankings — Dodgers Still Lead, Pirates Surge, Red Sox in Free Fall

x_posts.label: Post 7
x_posts.text:
MLB Power Rankings, Week 2:

Dodgers: Top of the league
Pirates: Rising fast (surprise!)
Yankees: Sliding after 2 straight losses
Red Sox: The basement

Two weeks in. Baseball is already messy. ⚾

#BallparkBanter #MLB #Baseball

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 10:30 PM ET*

Two weeks into the 2026 MLB season, we have some clarity on the baseball landscape — and some genuine surprises.

The Los Angeles Dodgers sit at the top of the power rankings and the standings, looking exactly like the team that was expected to contend for the World Series. Their depth — as Max Muncy proved with three home runs on Friday night — is real.

The New York Yankees started 8-2 with historically dominant pitching. They've since dropped two straight but remain well-positioned. The challenge of staying ahead without Gerrit Cole and Carlos Rodón in the rotation is the season-long storyline to watch.

The Pittsburgh Pirates are the week's biggest riser. Their early-season success has baseball's small-market fans paying attention. Can they sustain it against tougher competition in May and June?

And at the bottom: the Boston Red Sox. Their 2-8 start has been called "unacceptable" by their own manager. Until that trend reverses, they'll stay at the bottom of every ranking board.

Two weeks is too early to write anyone's obituary. But it's not too early to identify who looks real.
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with multi-team graphic*

Dodgers on top. Yankees sliding. Pirates rising. Red Sox at the bottom. Week 2 MLB Power Rankings tell the early story of 2026.
